title: The Authority and Sufficiency of the Scriptures - WCF Chapter 1 (Sections 4-6)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: The Authority and Sufficiency of the Scriptures - WCF Chapter 1 (sections 4-6) - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt
outline: The Bible's authority is self-authenticating, not dependent on church or human testimony (WCF 1.4)
Evidence for the reliability of Scripture: textual transmission, historical accuracy, scientific integrity
The uniqueness of the Bible: continuity, circulation, translation, survival, and teaching
The Holy Spirit's necessity for believing Scripture as the Word of God (WCF 1.5)
Introduction to the sufficiency of Scripture (WCF 1.6)
summary: This Sunday school lesson works through WCF Chapter 1, paragraphs 4-6, examining the authority and sufficiency of the Scriptures. Pastor Lyro teaches that the Bible's authority rests not on any church or person but on God himself as its author, and presents multiple lines of evidence for its reliability: unmatched textual transmission, confirmed historical accuracy through archaeology (including the Hittite Empire tablets and the Dead Sea Scrolls), freedom from scientific absurdity, and the unique continuity of a book written over 1,500 years by 40 authors across three continents. Despite all such evidence, the lesson emphasizes that only the inward work of the Holy Spirit can bring genuine belief that Scripture is the Word of God. The lesson closes with an introduction to the sufficiency of Scripture, which will be continued the following week.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Authority of Holy Scripture (WCF 1.4)

Last week we started a series alongside a study of Psalm 119, looking at what the whole Bible teaches about itself. That is really what the first chapter of our confession addresses, which makes sense since everything else is based on that. We have looked through paragraph 5 of chapter 1. Today we are going to look at paragraph 4 and, briefly, begin paragraph 6.

You will need a Bible and a hymnal. Turn to page 848 in the hymnal. We are considering the authority and sufficiency of the Bible.

As we saw, God has spoken in the past in various ways, but in these last days he has spoken through Jesus Christ. He has given us the canon of Scripture — the Bible — complete in 66 books. We don't need any other new revelation. This revelation is inspired by God, which means it is without error. Today we are going to see that this Scripture is authoritative and sufficient for everything concerning faith and practice.

Look at paragraph 4 on page 848:

> *The authority of the Holy Scripture, for which it ought to be believed and obeyed, dependeth not upon the testimony of any man or church, but wholly upon God (who is truth itself), the author thereof; and therefore it is to be received, because it is the Word of God.*

The Bible gets its authority not because some church or some person said so, but because the Bible itself stands on its own authority as the Word of God.

## Is the Bible the Word of God?

Many so-called Bible scholars have leveled attacks against the claim that the Bible is the Word of God. Because of these attacks, many churches that were once sound have fallen away. I hope you see how important this doctrine is. Everything stands on it. Every true Christian should believe that the Bible is the Word of God.

But in what ways is Scripture the Word of God? Many parts of the Bible directly quote God's statements — God speaking through Moses, the Lord Jesus Christ himself quoted in the New Testament. All these portions are clearly the Word of God. But what about the historical books — Joshua, Judges, 1 and 2 Samuel, 1 and 2 Kings — or the wisdom books like Job, Psalms, or Proverbs? Are they the Word of God?

To answer this question we must look at the testimony the Bible gives about its own origin. What does the Bible say about itself? You might say, "But that's circular reasoning." And I would say yes, it is — because the Bible is self-authenticating. It authenticates itself. It doesn't need outside testimony to be true.

> *"All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness."* (2 Timothy 3:16)

The Old Testament books witness to themselves. They say, "Thus says the Lord — you must obey." The books themselves expect people to follow their authority. Paul says the same thing in the New Testament: these are the words of God given to you. So the Bible expects people to receive it as the Word of God.

## Evidence That the Bible Is the Word of God

What I'm going to share next will be helpful for the believer but of no help to the unbeliever — and I will explain why at the end.

For a book to be considered to have come from God, it must meet certain requirements: it must be transmitted to us accurately; it must be correct when dealing with history; and it must not contain scientific absurdities or contradictions. All these things are true of the Bible.

### 1. Unique Textual Transmission

The Bible has been transmitted to us with greater accuracy than any other ancient document. There is more evidence for the reliability of the New Testament text than for any ten pieces of classic literature combined. Take Homer, Cicero, or any other classical writer — combine the ten most highly regarded — and there is *less* certainty about what they wrote than what we have in the New Testament.

Here is a striking fact: there is more certainty about the text of the New Testament than about the 37 plays of Shakespeare — plays that were *printed* in the late 1500s and early 1600s, after the invention of the printing press. That is how remarkable the preservation of God's Word is.

### 2. Historical Accuracy

For centuries critics have claimed the Bible is historically inaccurate, and every single time, the evidence has eventually vindicated Scripture.

For example, the name David had never been found outside the Bible until the 1990s. Then archaeologists excavating in Jerusalem found an inscription referring to David as the first king of the Jews. Surprise — the Bible had been saying that for 3,000 years.

In the 1970s a common argument against the Bible's reliability was that the Hittite Empire — described in the Bible as a massive empire comparable to Babylon or Assyria — left no archaeological trace. Therefore, critics said, the Bible must be false. But people kept digging. At a site in Syria called Ebla, archaeologists found thousands of cuneiform tablets — records of the Hittite Empire. Among them was a bill of sale: a receipt for property sold to a man named Abraham so he could bury his wife Sarah. The Bible had been saying that for 4,000 years.

There was also in the 1800s a man named Sir William Ramsay — an archaeologist and atheist who set out to disprove the book of Acts. He used Acts as a guide to navigate the Mediterranean and Palestine, intending to show that the historical details were false. By the end of his journey he had become a believer, because the historical accuracy of Acts led him to take the Bible seriously, and the Lord saved him.

### 3. No Scientific Absurdities

The Bible is not written as a science manual — and we praise the Lord for that, because science manuals are not riveting. Whatever the Bible says about scientific facts, however, is true.

People say the Bible groups whales with fish and bats with birds. But look at the actual language. The Bible says "creatures who live in the ocean" and "creatures who fly." Are we not fine saying that a bat and a bird are both creatures that fly? That's accurate.

The book of Job — considered by scholars to be among the oldest written books of the Old Testament — states that the earth is round, long before that was "discovered" by science. It also mentions the Pleiades star cluster, noting that its stars are bound together by their own power. This is remarkable: most constellations are not true gravitational clusters, but the Pleiades is one of the rare exceptions, held together by the gravitational pull of its own stars. Job knew this through divine revelation.

## The Uniqueness of the Bible

The Bible is not just a great book among great books. It is the greatest book. It is unique in every sense.

**Unique in its continuity.** The Bible was written over a 1,500-year period — from Moses in the 1400s BC to John writing Revelation in the 90s AD — spanning 40 generations, by more than 40 authors from every walk of life: a political and religious leader (Moses), a fisherman (Peter), a shepherd (Amos), a military general (Joshua), a cupbearer (Nehemiah), a prime minister of Babylon (Daniel), a doctor (Luke), a king (Solomon), a tax collector (Matthew), a rabbi (Paul). Written in different locations — the wilderness, a dungeon, exile on Patmos — on three continents (Asia, Africa, and Europe), in three languages (Hebrew, Aramaic, and Greek). And yet the Bible presents a unified continuity that no other book has.

Genesis 3:15 speaks of the seed of the woman who will crush the serpent's head. For 1,500 years, that one theme unfolds through books written without computers or databases, culminating in the return of that same seed in Revelation to judge all the world — driven entirely by the inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

**Unique in its circulation.** The Bible has been read by more people and published in more languages than any other book. In England alone, approximately 33,000 copies of the English Bible must be printed every day just to meet demand. No other book comes close.

**Unique in its translation.** As early as 250 BC, the Old Testament began to be translated into Greek. The whole Bible has now been translated into over 240 different languages and dialects — more than any other work, and not by a narrow margin.

**Unique in its survival.** No other book has been attacked as relentlessly as the Bible, and no other book has survived as consistently. Voltaire, the philosopher behind the French Revolution of 1789, declared that based on his enlightened ideas the Bible would no longer be published in France within his lifetime. Today his home is the headquarters of the French Bible Society.

**Unique in its teaching.** No other book presents prophecies so accurate in their fulfillment. The book of Daniel was long criticized as a forgery written after the events it described, because its predictions about Alexander the Great, the Persian kings, and the period between the Testaments are so precise. Scholars said no one could have written it before those events happened. Then in 1947 the Dead Sea Scrolls were discovered, providing indisputable copies of Daniel traceable to the second century BC — well before the events Daniel described. The prophecies were written *before* the events. They had to be.

## The Holy Spirit Is Necessary for Belief (WCF 1.5)

Here is the kicker. Despite all this evidence, no one will believe the Bible is the Word of God unless the Spirit of God opens their eyes. We could find Noah's ark. Moses could walk out of a tomb with the original stone tablets. And not one person would be added to the kingdom of God on that basis alone.

Why? Because the heavens already declare the glory of God. All creation already clearly declares the attributes of God, and sinful humanity willfully suppresses that truth. So we can argue to our faces turning blue; unless the Spirit of God supernaturally works, no one will believe — because everyone already knows God exists and is already suppressing that truth in unrighteousness.

Our confession says in paragraph 5:

> *We may be moved and induced by the testimony of the Church to an high and reverent esteem of the Holy Scripture... yet, notwithstanding, our full persuasion and assurance of the infallible truth and divine authority thereof, is from the inward work of the Holy Spirit, bearing witness by and with the Word in our hearts.*

We will never know the Word of God to be the Word of God unless the Spirit bears that witness to our hearts. Paul teaches this in 1 Corinthians 2:

> *"But God has revealed them to us through His Spirit. For the Spirit searches all things, yes, the deep things of God... Now we have received, not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God, that we might know the things that have been freely given to us by God."* (1 Corinthians 2:10, 12)

Apart from the Holy Spirit, the Bible cannot be properly understood unto salvation.

### Implications for Apologetics

This is really what the study of apologetics is all about — how do we defend the faith? And Scripture itself logically directs us: in our interaction with unbelievers, we should ground what we say in the Bible. Start with the Bible. The Bible's own foundational assumption is that everyone already knows there is a God. If that is true, we don't need to prove the existence of God — we just start with what the Bible says about the person and why they don't believe.

Remember how Paul spoke to the Athenians in Acts 17? He didn't spend time proving God exists. He said, "You have a notion of God. I can see that from all your idols. You just have the wrong notion. Let me tell you about the true God."

If you are called to give a defense for the faith, as Peter commands (1 Peter 3:15), the faith is so biblically based that you cannot do it apart from Scripture.

## Introduction to the Sufficiency of Scripture (WCF 1.6)

A lot of Christians say, "Yes, the Bible is the Word of God" — and then put it aside and live life according to something else. They affirm the authority of Scripture but don't find the Bible sufficient for life.

Our confession in paragraph 6 says:

> *The whole counsel of God, concerning all things necessary for His own glory, man's salvation, faith and life, is either expressly set down in Scripture, or by good and necessary consequence may be deduced from Scripture: unto which nothing at any time is to be added, whether by new revelations of the Spirit, or traditions of men.*

The Bible is sufficient for anything necessary for God's glory, man's salvation, what to believe, and how to live. Those four things: glorifying God, salvation, faith, and life. We will pick up here next week with the idea of the sufficiency of the Scriptures.
